text: The surgeon examines the surgical field, noting the appendix adhered to the right pelvic brim with the terminal ileum covering it. The anti-mesenteric fat of the ileum and cecum obscure the base of the appendix, which appears retrocecal. The surgeon prepares to mobilize the right colon and cecum for better visibility, using three laparoscopic ports: an umbilical Hassan, a 5mm suprapubic, and a 5mm left lower quadrant port.

The surgeon encounters a challenging adhesion on the pelvic brim, hindering the mobilization of the cecum from the sidewall. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon chips away at the adhesion, revealing the necrotic and inflamed wall of the appendix. Pus is visible as the surgeon continues to clean the area and attempts to gain a better view of the appendix base, which remains obscured. The inflamed tissue begins to separate more easily as the surgeon opens the covering rind and mobilizes the cecum further.

The surgeon observes the inflammatory reaction extending into the retroperitoneum while attempting to lift the cecum to visualize the retroperitoneal attachment of the appendix. Working on the medial surface, the surgeon searches for the anti-mesenteric fat to locate the appendix base. The surgeon decides to change the approach by taking the mesoappendix, aiming to reach the appendix base, potentially finding the open lumen, amputating the appendix, and suturing the hole closed. Care is taken to avoid the terminal ileum or cecum. The appendix disintegrates, revealing a fecalith, which is then removed.

The surgeon examines the open lumen in the cecum, observing the residual rind of the appendix adjacent to the terminal ileum insertion. The cecum appears damaged, prompting the surgeon to carefully remove part of the wall to access healthier tissue for suturing, while avoiding impingement on the terminal ileum.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, isolating the ileocolic pedicle. The ileocolic artery and vein are separated, followed by the thick and inflamed small bowel mesentery using a ligature. The suprapubic port is upsized to twelve millimeters for stapling and extraction purposes. The colon mesentery is preserved, except for the ileocolic section, and lateral attachments above the cecum at the junction with the ascending colon are taken.

The surgeon divides the terminal ileum using a stapler inserted through the suprapubic port. The hepatic flexure is mobilized before stapling the cecum and ascending colon. The camera is positioned at the suprapubic port, while stapling is performed from the umbilical port, requiring an additional firing. The specimen is placed above the liver.

The surgeon prepares for an isoperistaltic anastomosis by making an enterotomy and colotomy. The stapler is inserted through the suprapubic port, and a stay suture is used to align the bowel onto the stapler, connecting anti-mesenteric to anti-mesenteric surfaces to create a common channel. The common channel is then closed using a 3-0 V-Loc suture.

The surgeon completes the intracorporeal anastomosis, ensuring a flat mesentery with no bleeding from the staple line. The surgical field is cleaned, and the specimen is extracted through an upsized suprapubic port. The appendix site is checked, and a quick ureter inspection is performed.
